sections.heading: Background and Occasion
sections.html:
Background and Occasion

The First Council of Nicaea was convened by Emperor Constantine I in AD 325 at Nicaea in Bithynia (modern Iznik, Turkey). It was the first ecumenical council of the Christian church, attended by approximately 300 bishops, primarily from the eastern Mediterranean.

The immediate cause was the Arian controversy, sparked by the Alexandrian presbyter Arius, who taught that the Son of God was a created being — the first and greatest of God's creations, but not coeternal or coequal with the Father. His formula was: "There was a time when the Son was not." Arius drew large popular followings and his hymns spread the teaching widely.

Bishop Alexander of Alexandria condemned Arius and sought a universal ruling. Constantine, desiring unity in the empire, summoned the bishops to resolve the dispute. The council's principal theological spokesman against Arianism was the deacon Athanasius, who would spend the rest of his life defending its decisions.

The council also addressed the date of Easter (the Paschal controversy) and various matters of church discipline, producing twenty canons. But its enduring significance lies in the theological definition of Christ's nature.

sections.heading: The Nicene Creed and the Homoousios
sections.html:
The Nicene Creed and the Homoousios

The council's central achievement was the formulation of a creed — later expanded at Constantinople (381) into what we now call the Nicene Creed — and the insertion of the decisive term homoousios (ὁμοούσιος), meaning "of the same substance" or "consubstantial," to describe the Son's relation to the Father.

The original Nicene Creed (325) reads in part:

We believe in one God, the Father Almighty, Maker of all things visible and invisible. And in one Lord Jesus Christ, the Son of God, begotten of the Father, the only-begotten, that is, of the essence of the Father, God of God, Light of Light, very God of very God, begotten not made, of one substance with the Father [homoousios tō Patri], by whom all things were made both in heaven and on earth; who for us men, and for our salvation, came down and was incarnate and was made man; he suffered, and the third day he rose again, ascended into heaven; from thence he shall come to judge the quick and the dead. And in the Holy Ghost.

The council then appended explicit anathemas against Arian teaching:

But those who say: "There was a time when he was not," and "He was not before he was made," and "He was made out of nothing," or who assert that "the Son of God is of a different hypostasis or substance," or that "He is created," or "alterable," or "mutable" — the catholic and apostolic Church anathematizes them.

The term homoousios was controversial even among orthodox bishops because it did not appear in Scripture and had been used ambiguously before. But Athanasius and the majority recognized it as the only term that excluded every Arian evasion: if the Son shares the Father's very substance, he cannot be a lesser created being. (John 10:30; John 1:1–3; Col. 1:15–17; Heb. 1:3)

sections.heading: Selected Canons
sections.html:
Selected Canons

Beyond the creed, the council issued twenty canons governing church order. A selection of the most significant:

Canon 2. If any persons have lately come over to the faith from a heathen life, and have been taught for only a brief period, it does not seem right that they should immediately be ordained bishop, presbyter, or deacon. For the catechumen needs time, and after baptism further trial is requisite.

Canon 5. Concerning those who have been excommunicated, whether clergy or laity, the sentence should be examined by the bishops in each province — provided that no one is shut out of the church by other bishops' rashness or narrowness or harshness.

Canon 6. The ancient customs of Egypt, Libya, and Pentapolis shall be maintained, according to which the bishop of Alexandria has authority over all these places. For a similar relation exists in the case of the bishop of Rome. Similarly in Antioch and the other provinces, the churches are to retain their privileges.

Canon 7. Since custom and ancient tradition have prevailed that the bishop of Aelia [Jerusalem] should be honoured, let him, saving its due dignity to the Metropolitan [Caesarea], have the next place of honour.

Canon 13. Concerning the departing, the ancient canonical law is still to be maintained — namely, that those who are on the point of death shall not be deprived of the last and most necessary provision for the journey [the Eucharist].

Canon 20. Since there are some who kneel on the Lord's Day and during the days of Pentecost, this holy synod decrees that all offer their prayers to God standing, so that all things may be uniformly observed everywhere.
